content:
# John Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## 11:1 — "sick/ill" for Greek ασθενων (asthenen)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a certain [one] was sick" | Preserves Greek ασθενεω as "sick"; retains literal bracket notation for implied words |
| LOGOS | "a certain man was ill" | Formal equivalent; "ill" is slightly more elevated register than "sick" |
| PNEUMA | "A man named Lazarus was sick" | Restructures sentence to lead with Lazarus by name; natural English word order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** LOGOS and PNEUMA both use acceptable English equivalents. The structural divergence is more significant: EMET preserves the Greek word order where Lazarus is introduced second; PNEUMA front-loads his name for clarity. Consensus follows LOGOS phrasing ("a certain man was ill") preserving the Greek structure while using natural English.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:2 — "ointment/perfume" for Greek μυρου (myrou)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "ointment" | Standard literal rendering of μυρον |
| LOGOS | "ointment" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "expensive perfume" | Adds "expensive" (not in Greek text of this verse) for narrative color; uses "perfume" instead of "ointment" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's addition of "expensive" is an interpretive gloss drawn from John 12:3 (where the ointment is described as πολυτιμου). While accurate to the broader narrative, it is not present in the Greek of 11:2. Consensus retains "ointment" without the addition.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:3 — "he whom you love" vs. "your dear friend" for Greek ον φιλεις (hon phileis)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] whom you love is sick" | Literal with bracket notation |
| LOGOS | "he whom you love is ill" | Formal equivalent preserving relative clause |
| PNEUMA | "your dear friend is very sick" | Interprets φιλεω as friendship; adds "very" (not in Greek) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's "your dear friend" collapses the significant theological verb φιλεω into a noun phrase and loses the direct address. The addition of "very" is not supported by the Greek. Consensus preserves the relative clause structure. "He whom you love" maintains both the intimacy and the theological resonance of φιλεω.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:4 — "This illness/sickness" and structural divergence for Greek αυτη η ασθενεια
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This sickness is not unto death" | "Unto" preserves Greek προς with directional force |
| LOGOS | "This illness does not lead to death" | Interprets προς θανατον as purposive — "lead to" |
| PNEUMA | "Lazarus's sickness will not end in death" | Names Lazarus (not in Greek); "end in" is interpretive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** EMET's "unto" preserves the ambiguity of the Greek preposition προς. LOGOS's "lead to" and PNEUMA's "end in" both interpret the preposition, but in slightly different directions. PNEUMA's insertion of "Lazarus's" is not in the text. Consensus follows LOGOS for readability while preserving the sense.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:7 — "Judea/Ioudaia" — transliteration vs. translation
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ioudaia" | Transliterates Greek Ιουδαιαν; preserves foreignness |
| LOGOS | "Judea" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Judea" | Standard English rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** This is a systemic divergence across the entire translation. EMET consistently transliterates Ιουδαιοι/Ιουδαια to preserve the original sound and avoid anachronistic connotations. Consensus uses the standard anglicized "Judea" and "Jews" for readability, while noting EMET's rationale in the council notes.
**Status:** Resolved — systemic policy; adopted standard anglicization in consensus

---

## 11:8 — "the Jews/Ioudaioi/people in Judea" for Greek Ιουδαιοι
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ioudaioi" | Transliteration (see 11:7 above) |
| LOGOS | "the Jews" | Standard translation |
| PNEUMA | "the people in Judea" | Interprets Ιουδαιοι as geographic rather than ethnic/religious designation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's rendering "the people in Judea" is an interpretive choice that narrows the referent geographically. While this may be contextually appropriate here (the threat is local), it is not what the Greek says. In John's Gospel, Ιουδαιοι carries a complex range of meanings. Consensus uses "the Jews" with the understanding that John's usage is context-dependent.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:9-10 — Structural and interpretive divergence in the light/darkness metaphor
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he does not stumble, because he sees the light of this world... the light is not in him" | Literal; preserves "the light is not in him" (εν αυτω) |
| LOGOS | "he does not stumble, because he sees the light of this world... the light is not in him" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "During the day people can walk safely. They can see because they have the light of this world. But at night there is danger of stumbling because they have no light." | Paraphrases extensively; "they have no light" replaces "the light is not in him"; eliminates second person |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the theologically significant phrase "the light is not in him" (εν αυτω), which carries Johannine double meaning — referring both to physical light and to the indwelling divine light. The shift from "in him" to "they have no light" flattens the theology. Consensus preserves the literal phrasing.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:11 — "Lazarus the friend of us" vs. "Our friend Lazarus" for Greek Λαζαρος ο φιλος ημων
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lazaros the friend of us has fallen asleep" | Literal word order; preserves Greek genitive construction |
| LOGOS | "Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep" | Natural English possessive |
| PNEUMA | "Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Minor structural divergence. English naturally places the possessive before the noun. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:12 — "recover/be saved/get better" for Greek σωθησεται (sothesetai)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he will be saved" | Preserves the Greek σωζω which means both "save" and "heal" — theologically loaded |
| LOGOS | "he will recover" | Medical sense of σωζω; avoids theological overload in the disciples' misunderstanding |
| PNEUMA | "he will soon get better" | Colloquial paraphrase; adds "soon" (not in Greek) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** This is a significant theological divergence. The Greek σωζω carries dual meaning — physical recovery and spiritual salvation. EMET's "be saved" preserves this double meaning, which is characteristic of Johannine irony. LOGOS's "recover" is contextually appropriate for the disciples' misunderstanding but loses the deeper resonance. PNEUMA adds "soon" without textual warrant. Consensus uses "recover" for the surface reading, noting the σωζω wordplay in council notes.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus; noted EMET's insight in council notes

---

## 11:13 — "taking rest in sleep" vs. "sleep of the slumber" for Greek κοιμησεως του υπνου
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sleep of the slumber" | Hyper-literal rendering of κοιμησις + υπνος cognate pairing |
| LOGOS | "taking rest in sleep" | Smooth English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Lazarus was simply sleeping" | Free paraphrase; adds "simply" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek uses a redundant phrase (κοιμησεως του υπνου — literally "the falling-asleep of sleep") to emphasize the disciples' misunderstanding. EMET's rendering is awkward but captures the redundancy. LOGOS smooths it naturally. PNEUMA's "simply sleeping" captures the sense but loses the structure. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:15 — "I am glad/I rejoice" for Greek χαιρω (chairo)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I rejoice for you" | Literal rendering of χαιρω |
| LOGOS | "for your sake I am glad" | Formal equivalent; "glad" is softer than "rejoice" |
| PNEUMA | "I'm glad I wasn't there, for now you will really believe" | Adds "really" (not in Greek); uses contraction for conversational tone |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** EMET's "rejoice" and LOGOS's "glad" are both valid for χαιρω. "Rejoice" sounds stronger and potentially unsettling in context (glad about Lazarus's death?), which may be closer to the provocative force of the Greek. PNEUMA's "really" is an addition. Consensus uses "I am glad" as a middle path.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:16 — "die with him" — referent ambiguity
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Let us also go, that we may die with him" | Preserves ambiguity of "him" (Jesus or Lazarus?) |
| LOGOS | "Let us also go, that we may die with him" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Let's go, too — and die with Jesus" | Resolves ambiguity by naming Jesus explicitly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA resolves an ambiguity that the Greek deliberately preserves. The pronoun αυτου could refer to Jesus (going to dangerous Judea) or Lazarus (joining him in death). Most interpreters take it as Jesus, but the ambiguity is part of the Johannine texture. Consensus preserves the ambiguous "him."
**Status:** Resolved — adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:17 — "in the tomb/in his grave" for Greek εν τω μνημειω
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having four days in the tomb" | Literal; preserves Greek construction |
| LOGOS | "had already been in the tomb four days" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "had already been in his grave for four days" | "Grave" instead of "tomb"; adds possessive "his" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek μνημειον means "memorial/tomb" — a rock-cut chamber, not a dug grave. "Tomb" is more accurate to the archaeological and textual reality. PNEUMA's "grave" carries different connotations. Consensus uses "tomb."
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:18 — "fifteen stadia/two miles/a few miles" for Greek σταδιων δεκαπεντε
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "about fifteen stadia [away]" | Preserves original unit of measurement |
| LOGOS | "about two miles off" | Converts to modern equivalent (15 stadia ~ 1.7 miles, rounded) |
| PNEUMA | "only a few miles down the road from Jerusalem" | Vague approximation; adds "down the road" and "only" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** EMET preserves the original measurement, which requires a footnote for modern readers. LOGOS converts accurately. PNEUMA's "a few miles" is imprecise and "only" and "down the road" are additions. Consensus follows LOGOS with the modern conversion.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:20 — "remained seated/sat/stayed" for Greek εκαθεζετο (ekathezeto)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Maria sat in the house" | Literal rendering of the verb |
| LOGOS | "Mary remained seated in the house" | Adds "remained" to capture the imperfect aspect — she continued sitting |
| PNEUMA | "Mary stayed in the house" | Loses the specific posture; "stayed" is generic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek εκαθεζετο specifically means "was sitting" — the posture of mourning. LOGOS's "remained seated" best captures both the posture and the continuous aspect. PNEUMA's "stayed" loses the sitting detail. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:22 — "whatever you ask from God" vs. "whatever you ask God" for Greek οσα αν αιτηση τον θεον
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whatever you ask the God, the God will give you" | Literal; preserves double use of "the God" |
| LOGOS | "whatever you ask from God, God will give you" | Adds "from" for English idiom |
| PNEUMA | "God will give you whatever you ask" | Restructures entirely; front-loads God as subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Minor structural variation. EMET's double "the God" reflects the Greek article usage. LOGOS and the consensus use the standard English form. PNEUMA's restructuring loses Martha's emphasis pattern. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:25 — "Whoever believes/The one believing/Anyone who believes" for Greek ο πιστευων εις εμε
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] believing in me, even if he dies, will live" | Preserves participial construction |
| LOGOS | "Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live" | Formal; uses subjunctive "though he die" for elevated register |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who believes in me will live, even after dying" | Plain English; "even after dying" reframes the temporal relationship |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** LOGOS's rendering with the subjunctive "though he die, yet shall he live" captures the paradoxical force of the Greek and maintains appropriate theological register for this climactic "I am" statement. PNEUMA's "even after dying" is accurate but less rhetorically powerful. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:26 — "shall never die/will never ever die/will never die to the age" for Greek ου μη αποθανη εις τον αιωνα
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will never die to the age" | Preserves εις τον αιωνα literally ("into the age") |
| LOGOS | "shall never die" | Standard English; εις τον αιωνα treated as intensifier |
| PNEUMA | "will never ever die" | Colloquial doubling "never ever" to capture the emphatic ου μη + εις τον αιωνα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek uses double negation (ου μη) plus εις τον αιωνα for emphatic force. EMET's literal "to the age" is awkward. LOGOS's "shall never die" is elegant but may underrepresent the emphasis. PNEUMA's "never ever" captures the emphatic force colloquially. Consensus follows LOGOS but notes the emphatic construction.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:26 — Addition of "Martha" in direct address
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do you believe this?" | Matches Greek text |
| LOGOS | "Do you believe this?" | Matches Greek text |
| PNEUMA | "Do you believe this, Martha?" | Adds "Martha" — not in Greek text |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA adds Martha's name for dramatic effect, but it is not present in the Greek. Consensus omits it.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:27 — "the Christ/the Christos/the Messiah" for Greek ο Χριστος
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Christos" | Transliterates Greek title |
| LOGOS | "the Christ" | Standard English form of the Greek title |
| PNEUMA | "the Messiah" | Uses Hebrew-derived equivalent for accessibility |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Systemic divergence. "Christ" is the anglicized Greek; "Messiah" is the anglicized Hebrew (Mashiach). Both translate the same concept ("anointed one"). PNEUMA's choice connects to the Hebrew tradition; LOGOS preserves the Greek text's own term. Consensus uses "the Christ" as it directly reflects the Greek source text.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:27 — "I believe/I have believed" for Greek πεπιστευκα (pepistevka)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I have believed" | Preserves the Greek perfect tense — a past action with ongoing result |
| LOGOS | "I believe" | Renders the perfect as a present state (English idiom) |
| PNEUMA | "I have always believed" | Adds "always" to intensify the perfect tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek perfect πεπιστευκα indicates a settled, ongoing conviction — "I have come to believe and continue believing." EMET's "I have believed" is grammatically faithful. LOGOS's "I believe" captures the ongoing state. PNEUMA's "always" is an addition. Consensus uses "I believe" for natural English, noting the perfect tense.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:33 — "deeply moved/deeply moved in spirit/a deep anger welled up" for Greek ενεβριμησατο τω πνευματι (enebrimesato to pneumati)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was deeply moved in the spirit and troubled himself" | Preserves both verbs; retains "in the spirit" and reflexive "troubled himself" |
| LOGOS | "he was deeply moved in his spirit and greatly troubled" | Formal equivalent; adds "greatly" |
| PNEUMA | "a deep anger welled up within him, and he was deeply troubled" | Renders εμβριμαομαι as explicit anger; "welled up within him" is interpretive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** This is one of the most consequential divergences in the chapter. The Greek εμβριμαομαι literally means "to snort with anger" (used of horses). Traditional translations soften this to "deeply moved" or "groaned." PNEUMA's "a deep anger welled up" is closer to the lexical meaning but more interpretive. EMET and LOGOS use the standard "deeply moved" which, while familiar, may underrepresent the intensity. Consensus retains "deeply moved in his spirit and greatly troubled" for the translation text but affirms in the council notes that the Greek carries overtones of fury directed at death itself.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus; PNEUMA's insight preserved in council notes

---

## 11:34 — "laid/placed/put" for Greek τεθεικατε (tetheikate)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Where have you placed him?" | Literal rendering of τιθημι |
| LOGOS | "Where have you laid him?" | Traditional funeral language |
| PNEUMA | "Where have you put him?" | Colloquial |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Minor word choice divergence. "Laid" carries appropriate funeral register. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:37 — Structural divergence in the question about the blind man
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Was not this [one], the [one] having opened the eyes of the blind, able to do [something] that this [one] also would not die?" | Hyper-literal; preserves complex Greek syntax |
| LOGOS | "Could not he who opened the eyes of the blind man also have kept this man from dying?" | Formal English restructuring |
| PNEUMA | "This man healed a blind man. Couldn't he have kept Lazarus from dying?" | Splits into two sentences; names Lazarus (not in Greek); "healed" replaces "opened the eyes of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA simplifies significantly: "healed a blind man" loses the specific miracle detail ("opened the eyes"), and naming Lazarus is an addition. LOGOS preserves the single-sentence structure and the specific reference to opening eyes. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:38 — "deeply moved again/still angry" for Greek εμβριμωμενος εν εαυτω
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "again being deeply moved in himself" | Literal; "in himself" preserves εν εαυτω |
| LOGOS | "deeply moved again" | Concise formal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus was still angry" | Explicit anger; "still" implies continuity from v.33 |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's "still angry" is interpretively bold — it reads εμβριμαομαι as anger (lexically defensible) and "still" as continuity from v.33. EMET/LOGOS use the conventional "deeply moved." Consensus follows LOGOS while noting the anger interpretation in council notes.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus; PNEUMA's interpretation noted

---

## 11:39 — "there will be an odor/he stinks/the smell will be terrible" for Greek ηδη οζει (ede ozei)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "already he stinks, for it is [the] fourth day" | Literal and blunt; preserves Greek directness |
| LOGOS | "by this time there will be an odor, for he has been dead four days" | Formal; softens the language with "there will be an odor" |
| PNEUMA | "he has been dead for four days. The smell will be terrible" | Splits into two sentences; "terrible" is an addition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek οζει is a present tense verb meaning "he stinks" — blunt and direct. EMET captures this force. LOGOS softens it to "there will be an odor," which shifts to future tense and euphemistic language. PNEUMA's "the smell will be terrible" adds interpretive intensification. Consensus follows LOGOS for register consistency, but EMET's directness better represents the Greek.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus; noted EMET's fidelity to Greek directness

---

## 11:43 — "cried out with a loud voice/shouted" for Greek φωνη μεγαλη εκραυγασεν
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with a great voice he cried out" | Literal word order |
| LOGOS | "he cried out with a loud voice" | Standard English reordering |
| PNEUMA | "Jesus shouted" | Condensed; loses "with a loud voice" as separate phrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's "shouted" is economical but loses the deliberate emphasis of φωνη μεγαλη ("with a great/loud voice"), which signals the authoritative command. Consensus preserves the full phrase.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:44 — "linen strips/wrappings/graveclothes" for Greek κειριαις (keiriais)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the feet and the hands having been bound with wrappings" | Literal; generic "wrappings"; note reversed order (feet before hands) |
| LOGOS | "his hands and feet bound with linen strips" | Specifies material ("linen"); standard hand-feet order |
| PNEUMA | "his hands and feet bound in graveclothes" | "Graveclothes" is a compound interpretation; broader term |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek κειριαι refers to burial wrappings/bandages. LOGOS's "linen strips" specifies the material (accurate to burial practice). PNEUMA's "graveclothes" is broader but acceptable. EMET's word order (feet then hands) follows the Greek; LOGOS/PNEUMA normalize to English convention. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:44 — "Unbind/Loose/Unwrap" for Greek λυσατε (lysate)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Loose him and let him go" | Traditional rendering of λυω |
| LOGOS | "Unbind him, and let him go" | Formal; "unbind" matches the binding imagery |
| PNEUMA | "Unwrap him and let him go!" | "Unwrap" connects to the graveclothes; adds exclamation mark |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three are valid renderings of λυω. "Unbind" best pairs with the "bound" (δεδεμενος) of the same verse. PNEUMA's exclamation mark is editorial. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:47 — "performs many signs/does many signs/performs many miraculous signs" for Greek πολλα ποιει σημεια
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this man does many signs" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "this man performs many signs" | Formal; "performs" elevates register |
| PNEUMA | "This man certainly performs many miraculous signs" | Adds "certainly" and "miraculous" — neither in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA adds two words not in the text: "certainly" (intensifier) and "miraculous" (interpretive qualifier for σημεια). The Greek σημεια means "signs" — John deliberately avoids calling them "miracles" (δυναμεις), preferring the semiotic term. Consensus preserves "signs" without addition.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:48 — "our holy place/the place of us/our temple" for Greek τον τοπον (ton topon)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the place of us" | Hyper-literal; τοπος = "place" |
| LOGOS | "our holy place" | Interprets τοπος as the Temple; adds "holy" |
| PNEUMA | "our temple" | Explicitly identifies τοπος as the Temple |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek τοπος here likely refers to the Temple (a common Jewish circumlocution), though it could also mean the nation's place/land. EMET preserves the ambiguity. LOGOS adds "holy" which is not in the text but clarifies the referent. PNEUMA makes the identification explicit. Consensus follows LOGOS with "our holy place" as a middle path.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:48 — "everyone/all" for Greek παντες (pantes)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all will believe in him" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "everyone will believe in him" | Standard English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "soon everyone will believe in him" | Adds "soon" — not in Greek |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's "soon" is an addition that implies temporal urgency not present in the Greek. Consensus omits it.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:49 — "You know nothing at all/You know nothing/You don't know what you're talking about" for Greek υμεις ουκ οιδατε ουδεν
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You know nothing" | Literal; concise |
| LOGOS | "You know nothing at all" | Adds "at all" for emphasis matching ουδεν |
| PNEUMA | "You don't know what you're talking about!" | Free paraphrase; adds specificity and exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek ουκ οιδατε ουδεν uses double negation for emphasis. LOGOS's "at all" captures this emphatic force. PNEUMA's paraphrase changes the meaning — "you don't know what you're talking about" is different from "you know nothing." Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:50 — "it is better/it is expedient" for Greek συμφερει (sympherei)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it is better for you" | Renders συμφερω as "better" |
| LOGOS | "it is better for you" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "it's better for you" | Same meaning with contraction |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** No meaningful divergence here. All three agree on "better." The Greek συμφερω more precisely means "it is expedient/advantageous" — a colder, more calculating word than "better." But all three converge.
**Status:** Resolved — consensus matches all three

---

## 11:51 — "of his own accord/from himself/on his own" for Greek αφ εαυτου (aph heautou)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not from himself" | Literal; preserves preposition |
| LOGOS | "not of his own accord" | Formal idiomatic expression |
| PNEUMA | "not on his own" | Colloquial; could imply he had help rather than prophetic inspiration |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA's "not on his own" could be misread as "he had collaborators" rather than "he spoke by divine inspiration." LOGOS's "not of his own accord" better captures the involuntary prophetic dimension. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

## 11:52 — "gather into one/bring together and unite" for Greek συναγαγη εις εν
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the children of the God who have been scattered he might gather into one" | Literal; preserves Greek word order with verb at end |
| LOGOS | "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad" | Formal; adds "abroad" |
| PNEUMA | "to bring together and unite all the children of God scattered around the world" | Doubles the verb ("bring together and unite"); adds "all" and "around the world" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA adds three elements not in the Greek: the doubled verb, "all," and "around the world." While these capture the theological implication, they expand beyond the text. LOGOS's "abroad" is a minor interpretive addition for "scattered" (διεσκορπισμενα). Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

## 11:53 — "made plans to put him to death/planned that they might kill him/began to plot Jesus' death" for Greek εβουλευσαντο ινα αποκτεινωσιν αυτον
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they planned that they might kill him" | Literal; preserves the subjunctive purpose clause |
| LOGOS | "they made plans to put him to death" | Formal equivalent; "put him to death" is more deliberate than "kill" |
| PNEUMA | "the Jewish leaders began to plot Jesus' death" | Specifies "Jewish leaders" (not in Greek here); "began to plot" adds inception; names Jesus |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA adds specificity ("Jewish leaders," "Jesus") not in the immediate Greek text and adds "began to" which implies inception. The Greek simply says "they resolved/planned." LOGOS's "put him to death" better captures the deliberate, judicial nature of βουλευω. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:54 — "town/city/village" for Greek πολιν (polin)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a city called Ephraim" | Literal rendering of πολις |
| LOGOS | "a town called Ephraim" | Downgrades "city" to "town" given Ephraim's small size |
| PNEUMA | "the village of Ephraim" | Further downgrades to "village" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek πολις can refer to any settled community, from village to metropolis. Ephraim was a small settlement. LOGOS's "town" is a reasonable contextual rendering. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:54 — "walked openly/stopped his public ministry" for Greek παρρησια περιεπατει
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "no longer walked openly among the Ioudaioi" | Literal rendering of παρρησια + περιπατεω |
| LOGOS | "no longer walked openly among the Jews" | Same as EMET with anglicized names |
| PNEUMA | "stopped his public ministry among the people and left Jerusalem" | Interprets "walking openly" as "public ministry"; adds "left Jerusalem" (not in Greek) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA interprets the physical metaphor ("walked openly") as an abstract concept ("public ministry") and adds "left Jerusalem" which, while implied, is not in the text. Consensus preserves the concrete imagery of EMET/LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:56 — "That he will not come to the feast at all?" for Greek ου μη ελθη εις την εορτην
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "That he will not come to the feast?" | Literal; omits "at all" |
| LOGOS | "That he will not come to the feast at all?" | Adds "at all" to capture emphatic ου μη |
| PNEUMA | "He won't come for Passover, will he?" | Tag question format; specifies "Passover" instead of "feast" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The Greek ου μη is an emphatic negation. LOGOS's "at all" captures this force. PNEUMA's tag question changes the rhetorical structure. Specifying "Passover" is accurate (from context) but the Greek says εορτην ("feast"). Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:57 — "arrest/seize" for Greek πιασωσιν (piasosin)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "seize him" | Literal; πιαζω = to seize, catch |
| LOGOS | "arrest him" | Legal/formal term |
| PNEUMA | "arrest him" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Minor divergence. "Seize" is more physical; "arrest" implies legal authority. The Greek πιαζω is closer to "seize/catch" but the context is legal. Consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA with "arrest."
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

---

## 11:57 — "let them know/report it/report it immediately" for Greek μηνυση (menyse)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he should report [it]" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "he should let them know" | Idiomatic English |
| PNEUMA | "must report it immediately so they could arrest him" | Adds "immediately" and "must" (stronger obligation than Greek subjunctive) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** PNEUMA adds "immediately" and strengthens the modal from subjunctive to imperative ("must"). The Greek uses a subjunctive which indicates purpose, not urgency. Consensus follows LOGOS.
**Status:** Resolved — adopted LOGOS rendering in consensus

**Total divergences identified:** 35
**Resolved in favor of EMET:** 0 (though EMET's fidelity noted in several entries)
**Resolved in favor of LOGOS:** 30 (LOGOS rendering adopted or matched consensus)
**Resolved as EMET/LOGOS agreement:** 5
**PNEUMA insights preserved in council notes:** 2 (vv.33, 38 — anger interpretation of ενεβριμησατο)

**General pattern:** EMET provides hyper-literal renderings that expose Greek structure but sometimes produce awkward English. LOGOS consistently delivers the formal-equivalent balance that anchors the consensus. PNEUMA contributes vivid, accessible readings but frequently adds words not in the Greek text (approximately 15 additions identified) and resolves ambiguities the Greek preserves. PNEUMA's strongest contribution is the interpretation of ενεβριμησατο as anger rather than gentle sorrow — a lexically defensible and theologically important reading preserved in the council notes.
